title: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
author: Rolley Haggard
orientation: Rolley Haggard tells a brief Christmas-eve parable in which an unborn child cries out from a dim corner of a sanctuary and asks the gathered church to intervene against an impending abortion; when the congregation pleads that they have no scriptural commandment to act, the child finally asks them to regard him as an enemy, since the Lord has plainly commanded, 'love your enemies.' The piece functions as a sharp rebuke to churches who hide behind alleged scriptural silence to justify inaction against abortion.
audiences: Pew-sitting Christians who consider themselves pro-life
Skeptics who view the church as indifferent to abortion
Theologians and ethicists weighing scriptural arguments

questions.question: What setting does Haggard choose for his parable, and why does the timing matter?
questions.answer: He stages the encounter on Christmas, during the candlelight service, just as the gathered church is about to receive the benediction. By placing the unborn child's cry inside the very moment Christians celebrate the Incarnation, Haggard pointedly contrasts the church's worship of the Word-made-flesh with their refusal to defend other unborn children.
questions.quote: It was Christmas, and the church was gathered for the candlelight service. The carols were sung, the prayers were said, the sermon was preached. But as the people bowed their heads for the benediction, there came from a dim corner the sound of one weeping, though no one could be seen.

questions.question: What reason does the church give for refusing to intervene on the unborn child's behalf?
questions.audience: Pew-sitting Christians who consider themselves pro-life
questions.answer: The congregation, after whispered discussion, claims they cannot find a specific biblical instruction that addresses the unborn child's situation. This appeal to alleged scriptural silence becomes the parable's central indictment of churches that demand explicit commandments before acting against injustice.
questions.quote: After more whispered discussion they answered, “No, child. We can find no specific instruction in the Scripture regarding such cases.”

questions.question: How does the child interpret the church's refusal, and how does the church respond?
questions.audience: Skeptics who view the church as indifferent to abortion
questions.answer: The child reads inaction as hatred and asks what he has done to deserve it. The church protests that they do not hate him, exposing the gap between their self-perception and the moral weight of their refusal to love a neighbor in mortal danger.
questions.quote: The child wept bitterly. “Why do you hate me? What have I done?”

questions.question: How does the church narrow the scope of the love command to excuse itself?
questions.audience: Theologians and ethicists weighing scriptural arguments
questions.answer: They claim that nowhere in Scripture are they expressly commanded to love those still in the womb, treating the absence of a verse that names the unborn as a license for inaction. Haggard exposes this as a theologically dishonest move that demands hyper-specific commandments rather than applying the plain moral logic of Scripture.
questions.quote: “Dear child”, they said, “it is simply that we have no clear commandment from the Lord. Nowhere are we expressly bidden to love those yet in the womb.”

questions.question: What is the unexpected request the still, small voice finally makes?
questions.answer: After falling silent, the child asks the church to regard him as their enemy. It is a devastating reversal: he would rather be hated under a clear commandment than ignored under a manufactured one, because hatred would at least obligate the church to act.

questions.question: What scriptural commandment does the child invoke to corner the church's reasoning?
questions.answer: He cites Jesus' explicit command to love one's enemies. By framing himself as an enemy, the child traps the church's own logic: if they will not love him as a neighbor for lack of a verse, they are still bound by the commandment to love enemies, and so their refusal to act has no excuse left.
questions.quote: “Because then you would have clear commandment, for the Lord said, ‘*love your enemies*.’”

questions.question: Why does the parable describe the child's voice as a 'still, small voice,' and what does that allusion accomplish?
questions.audience: Theologians and ethicists weighing scriptural arguments
questions.answer: The phrase echoes the prophet Elijah's encounter with God on Horeb, where the Lord came not in wind or fire but in a still, small voice. By giving that biblical idiom to the unborn child, Haggard implies that God himself is speaking through the child's plea, and that the church's deafness to abortion is deafness to God.
questions.quote: But then, just as the people were about to turn away, a still, small voice said through fresh tears, “then will you do this one thing for me?”

related.title: It's All About the Church
related.reason: Argues that the local church is the indispensable engine of abolition, providing the constructive counterpart to Haggard's rebuke of churches that refuse to act.

related.title: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
related.reason: A direct call for congregations to mobilize for abolition, addressed to the same audience the parable indicts.

related.title: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
related.reason: Haggard's companion Christmas-season piece grounding pro-life action in the Incarnation, the same theological setting his parable exploits.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Another short Haggard piece skewering excuses for delay, complementing the parable's attack on churches that defer action.

related.title: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
related.reason: A concrete case study of church-affiliated leaders deploying the very kind of evasive scriptural reasoning the parable mocks.

related.title: Kristan Hawkins' Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
related.reason: Tackles head-on the move of using selective scriptural reasoning to justify inaction against abortion, which is the parable's central target.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Extends the parable's implicit call to repentance for church inaction, urging abolitionists themselves to model the obedience they demand of others.
